elements.sent-2411-001: Black hole accretion disks are a fascinating topic in astrophysics, as they play a crucial role in several high-energy situations.
elements.sent-2411-002: This paper investigates the optical appearance of scalar hairy black holes (SHBHs) with asymmetric potential, a numerical solution obtained in Phys.. Rev.
elements.sent-2411-004: Since the solution is spherically symmetric and surrounded by a thin accretion disk, we base our analysis on the work of J.~P.. Lumininet (1979).
elements.sent-2411-005: We discuss the behavior of the effective potential for massive and massless particles, the innermost stable circular orbits (ISCO), and the photon sphere radius for different SHBHs.
elements.sent-2411-006: The study includes the plots of isoradial curves and spectral shifts arising from gravitational and Doppler shifts by considering direct and secondary images.
elements.sent-2411-007: Based on the work of Page and Thorne (1974), we also investigate the intrinsic intensity of radiation emitted by the disk at a given radius, which allows the calculation of the distribution of observed bolometric flux.
elements.sent-2411-008: We use the angular size of the shadow reported by the EHT for Sagittarius A* and M87* to constrain the SHBHs parameters.
elements.sent-2411-009: The black holes predicted by general relativity (GR) are characterized uniquely by the mass, the angular momentum, and the electric charge; the well-known non-hair theorem/conjecture~\cite{Israel:1967wq, Israel:1967za, Carter:1971zc, Hawking:1972qk, Robinson:1975bv .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The conjecture considers two assumptions: firstly, the existence of an event horizon that encloses the singularity~\cite{Penrose:1969pc , and secondly, there are no closed time-like loops in the exterior domain of the black hole~\cite{Johannsen:2010ru .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Nevertheless, when it comes to astrophysical black holes, the presence of the plasma around them quickly discharges the BHs, and the electric charge is usually neglected~\cite{Zajacek:2019kla .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore, scientists believe that astrophysical black holes are best modeled by the Kerr space-time, which only considers the mass and the angular momentum, the Kerr hypothesis~\cite{Bambi:2011mj .}
elements.sent-2411-013: On the other hand, some research works have demonstrated the possibility of eluding the non-hair theorem/conjecture under special conditions~\cite{Bizon:1990sr, Bartnik:1988am .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In~\cite{Bizon:1990sr , for example, P.~Bizon numerically investigated the static and spherically symmetric Einstein-Yang-Mills equations with $SU(2)$ gauge group showing that these equations have asymptotically flat solutions with regular event horizons that approximates the Schwarzschild solution.}
elements.sent-2411-015: A similar analysis was performed previously by R.~Bartnik and J.~McKinnon in~\cite{Bartnik:1988am , where the authors also show the existence of non-singular and asymptotically flat numerical solutions that, in a near-field region, behave approximately to the Reissner-Nordstrom with Dirac monopole curvature source and, asymptotically, behave as Schwarzschild space-time.}
elements.sent-2411-016: According to P.~Bizon, ``the existence of these solutions is incompatible with the idea of the no-hair conjecture since the Yang-Mills hair is not associated with any global charge which would forbid it to be radiated away to infinity.''~\cite{Bizon:1990sr .}
elements.sent-2411-017: There are more examples of numerical black hole solutions violating the no-hair theorem/conjecture in~\cite{Kuenzle:1990is, Greene:1992fw, Lavrelashvili:1992ia, Corichi:2005pa, Chew:2022enh, Chew:2023olq .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Nevertheless, some of these solutions are not stable~\cite{Straumann:1989tf, Straumann:1990as, Bizon:1991nt, Zhou:1991nu .}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this sense, although the non-hair theorem/conjecture only involves the existence of solutions and not their stability, this is still a crucial question to consider when obtaining these kinds of solutions~\cite{Greene:1992fw .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Another way to construct numerical solutions not compatible with the no-hair theorem/conjecture is to minimally couple the Einstein gravity with a scalar field in which the scalar potential is not strictly positive so that the weak energy condition is violated~\cite{Herdeiro:2015waa .}
elements.sent-2411-021: One example of such construction was proposed in~\cite{Corichi:2005pa .}
elements.sent-2411-022: There, the authors solved numerically the field equations for static solutions of a self-gravitating scalar field with an asymmetric scalar potential containing a global minimum, a local minimum, and a local maximum.
elements.sent-2411-023: In this way, A.~Corichi et al. construct the asymptotically flat black hole solution by considering the local minimum of the scalar potential to be zero and then analyzing the empirical mass formulas, finding that the ADM mass of the space-time is similar to other ``hairy'' theories for small black holes, but its behavior changes drastically in the case of larger black holes.
elements.sent-2411-024: Similar solutions following this procedure were obtained in \cite{Nucamendi:1995ex, Karakasis:2021rpn, Anabalon:2012ih, Nikonov:2008zz, Winstanley:2005fu, Martinez:2004nb, Bronnikov:2001ah, Dennhardt:1996cz, Bechmann:1995sa .}
elements.sent-2411-025: From the observational point of view, we have seen strong evidence of the existence of black holes in the universe from different perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-026: On the one hand, we have the observation of the dynamics of stars near the center of our galaxy (Sgr A*) showing that the central object is undoubtedly a supermassive black hole~\cite{Ghez:1998ph, Ghez:2000ay, Ghez:2003rt, Ghez:2008ms .}
elements.sent-2411-027: On the other hand, we have the data from the gravitational wave observatories LIGO-VIRGO~\cite{LIGOScientific:2016aoc and the observations from the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) of the supermassive black holes M87*~\cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2019dse and Sagittarius A*~\cite{EventHorizonTelescope:2022wkp .}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In this sense, the possibility of testing the non-hair theorem/conjecture has increased in the last decades.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this sense, there has been relevant research work on potential tests of the no-hair theorem using gravitational waves~\cite{Ryan:1995wh, Ryan:1997kh, Ryan:1997hg, Datta:2019euh or the electromagnetic spectrum~\cite{Johannsen:2010ru, Glampedakis:2023eek .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this work, we use the numerical black hole solution obtained by A.~Corichi et al. in \cite{Corichi:2005pa to investigate the image cast by this scalar hairy black hole (SHBH) using a ray tracing code.}
elements.sent-2411-031: We organize the paper as follows: in Sec.~\ref{SecII , we discuss the most important aspects and considerations for obtaining the numerical black hole solution.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Then, in Sec.~\ref{secIII , we introduce the Hamiltonian formalism and obtain the equations of motion needed in the ray tracing code.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In this section, we also define the effective potential for both photons and massive particles, from which we obtain the photon sphere radius ($r_\text{ps}$) and the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO).
elements.sent-2411-034: In Sec.~\ref{secIV , we focus on the images of SHBHs.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We divide the section into two subsections, where we investigate the behavior of the angular diameter of the shadow, the specific intensity, the accretion disk, the photon rings, the redshift factor, and the energy flux, comparing our results with the Schwarzschild black hole.
elements.sent-2411-036: When analyzing the shadow and the photon rings, we consider a static, spherically symmetric, optically, and geometrically thin accretion flow, following Refs.~\cite{Zeng:2020dco, Jaroszynski:1997bw, Bambi:2013nla .}
elements.sent-2411-037: On the other hand, when considering the redshift, the energy flux, and the image of the SHBH, we assume a thin accretion disk on the equatorial plane using the same physical setup as Luminent~\cite{Luminet1979 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Finally, in Sec.~\ref{secV , we discuss our results.}

elements.sent-2411-065: Different properties of the SHBH solutions, such as Hawking temperature $T_H$, the area of the horizon $A_H$, the Ricci and the Kretschmann scalars, $R$ and $K$, respectively, are investigated in detail in~\cite{Chew:2022enh .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Finally, it is important to mention that the space-time studied in this paper corresponds to an example of an asymptotically flat black hole with scalar hair that is regular on and outside the event horizon~\cite{Herdeiro:2015waa .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The main idea of this solution, inspired by the isolated horizons (IH) formalism, is to consider space-times with an interior boundary (the horizon), satisfying quasi-local boundary conditions and ensuring that the horizon remains ``isolated''.
elements.sent-2411-068: While the boundary conditions are rooted in geometric considerations, they give rise to a well-defined action principle and Hamiltonian framework.
elements.sent-2411-069: Furthermore, these boundary conditions suggest that specific ``quasi-local charges'' determined at the horizon remain constant ``over time''; therefore, one can interpret these quasi-local charges as similar to the global charges defined at infinity in the context of asymptotically flat space-times~\cite{Corichi:2005pa .}
elements.sent-2411-070: It is well-known that Bekenstein developed a method for proving the inexistence of scalar hair~\cite{Bekenstein:1972ny, Bekenstein:1971hc, Bekenstein:1972ky , which contains three key assumptions: 1) the type of scalar field equation; 2) the space-time symmetry inheritance by the scalar field and 3) an energy condition~\cite{Herdeiro:2015waa .}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Although violating one of the assumptions does not guarantee the existence of a regular BH with scalar hair, one can find some explicit solutions in Ref.~\cite{Herdeiro:2015waa .}
elements.sent-2411-072: In particular, the solution proposed by Corichi et al. violates the third assumption, i.e., the weak energy condition (WEC).
elements.sent-2411-073: From the physical point of view, the violation of the WEC means that the asymmetric potential is not positive-define, ``providing an extra repulsive interaction and, therefore, preventing the scalar field from infinitely piling up at the (would be) horizon when one requires $\phi$ to be non-trivial~\cite{Herdeiro:2015waa .}
elements.sent-2411-074: In this sense, the violation of the WEC is necessary in the horizon to ensure a regular solution on and outside it.
elements.sent-2411-075: On the other hand, as pointed out by C.~Hereido~\cite{Herdeiro:2015waa , although there is not yet a clear physical setting for the WEC violation on the horizon, at least in the asymptotically flat case, the assumption of it leads to BHs with scalar hair possessing distinct properties.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Hence, from the astrophysical perspective, it is worth exploring and investigating these solutions from the observational point of view.
elements.sent-2411-077: In particular, as we show in this work, we explore the optical appearance of a numerical SHBH solution, investigate the shadow and the profile of the specific intensity, and use the EHT observation to constrain the parameter $\phi_0$.
elements.section-2411-003: The Hamiltonian formalism and the Equation of motion\label{secIII}
elements.sent-2411-078: To generate and investigate the image of scalar hairy black holes (SHBHs), we need to describe the motion of massive and massless particles in this spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-079: Usually, one models the particle motion by a system of differential equations obtained using the Hamiltonian Formalism (HF) because, according to Ref.~\cite{Levin:2008mq , numerical difficulties such as error propagation when solving the equations of motion, can be avoided naturally in this formalism.}
elements.sent-2411-080: We begin by considering a general static and spherically symmetric spacetime, given by the line element \begin{equation}ds^2= g_{tt}dt^2+g_{rr}dr^2+g_{\theta\theta}d\theta^2+g_{\varphi\varphi}d\varphi^2,\label{s3e1}\end{equation} where we assume that the metric components only depend on the coordinates $r$ and $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-081: The Lagrangian describing the dynamics of a test particle moving in this spacetime is given by \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2} g_{\mu\nu}\dot{x}^\mu \dot{x}^\nu = -\frac{\epsilon}{2}, \label{eq:Lagrangian}\end{equation} where $\epsilon=0$ and $\epsilon=1$ refer to massless and massive particles, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-082: In this expression, we use a dot to denote the derivative with respect to the affine parameter $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-083: The canonical 4-momentum is defined as \begin{equation}\label{s3e3} p_\alpha\equiv\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{x}^\alpha}}=g_{\alpha\beta}\dot{x}^\beta=g_{\alpha\beta}p^\beta.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-084: For the spacetime in Eq.~\eqref{s3e1 , the components of the canonical momentum are \begin{equation}\label{s3e4} \begin{aligned} p_t =\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{t}}}=g_{tt}\dot{t} p_r =\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{r}}}=g_{rr}\dot{r} p_\theta =\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\theta}}}=g_{\theta\theta}\dot{\theta} p_\varphi =\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial{\dot{\varphi}}}=g_{\varphi\varphi}\dot{\varphi}. \end{aligned}\end{equation} It is straightforward to show that the Hamiltonian and the Lagrangian are equal, \begin{equation}\label{s3e5} \mathcal{H}=p_\mu\dot{x}^\mu-\mathcal{L}=\frac{1}{2}g^{\alpha\beta}p_\alpha p_\beta=\mathcal{L},\end{equation} and constant~\cite{Chandrasekhar:1998 .}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\label{s3e4}  p_t =\frac{\partial L}{\partial{\dot{t}}}=g_{tt}\dot{t} p_r =\frac{\partial L}{\partial{\dot{r}}}=g_{rr}\dot{r} p_\theta =\frac{\partial L}{\partial{\dot{\theta}}}=g_{\theta\theta}\dot{\theta} p_\varphi =\frac{\partial L}{\partial{\dot{\varphi}}}=g_{\varphi\varphi}\dot{\varphi}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-085: Hence, the equations of motion for the coordinates $x^\mu$ and the momenta $p_\mu$, in the Hamiltonian formalism, are given by the relations \begin{equation}\label{s3e6} \begin{array}{ccc} \dot{x}^\mu=\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial p_\mu} \text{ and } \dot{p}_\mu=-\frac{\partial \mathcal{H}}{\partial x^\mu}. \end{array}\end{equation} For the general line element in Eq.~\eqref{s3e1 , we obtain the following system of eight differential equations: \begin{equation}\label{s3e7} \begin{aligned} \dot{t}= g^{tt}p_t \dot{r}= g^{rr}p_r \dot{\theta}= g^{\theta\theta}p_\theta \dot{\varphi}= g^{\varphi\varphi}p_\varphi \dot{p}_t= 0 \dot{p}_r= -\frac{1}{2}\big(p^2_t\partial_r g^{tt}+p^2_r\partial_rg^{rr}+p^2_\theta\partial_rg^{\theta\theta}+p^2_\varphi \partial_rg^{\varphi\varphi}. \big) \dot{p}_\theta= -\frac{1}{2}\big(p^2_t\partial_\theta g^{tt}+p^2_r\partial_\theta g^{rr}+p^2_\theta\partial_\theta g^{\theta\theta}+p^2_\varphi \partial_\theta g^{\varphi\varphi}\big) \dot{p}_\varphi= 0. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Note that $p_t$ and $p_\varphi$ are constants of motion associated with the energy, $E$, and the angular momentum, $L$, respectively.}

elements.sent-2411-086: Therefore, there are two Killing vector fields, $\xi=\partial_t$ and $\zeta=\partial_\varphi$ from which \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} -E = p_t =g_{tt}\dot{t} L =p_\varphi =g_{\varphi\varphi}\dot{\varphi},\label{eq:ConservedQuantities} \end{aligned}\end{equation} and, $\dot{t}$ and $\dot{\varphi}$ can be written in the form \begin{equation}\label{s3e9} \begin{aligned} \dot{t} =-g^{tt}E \dot{\varphi} =g^{\varphi\varphi}L. \end{aligned}\end{equation} In the case of SHBH given by Eq.~\eqref{eq:LineElement and restricting the analysis to the equatorial plane, $\theta=\pi/2$, so that $\dot{\theta}$ and $\dot{p _\theta$ vanish, the system \eqref{s3e7 reduces to \begin{equation}\label{s3e10} \begin{aligned} \dot{t}= e^{2 \sigma}N^{-1}E \dot{r}= Np_r \dot{\varphi}= \frac{L}{r^2} \dot{p}_r= \frac{L^2}{r^3}-\frac{1}{2} p^2_r N'-\frac{E^2 e^{2 \sigma} \left[N'-2 N \sigma'\right]}{2 N^2} \end{aligned}\end{equation} while the conserved quantities become \begin{equation}E = e^{-2 \sigma} N \dot{t} \,, \quad L = r^2 \dot{\varphi} \,.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-139: To investigate the shadow of hairy black holes, we start by defining the impact parameter of photons as \begin{equation}\label{eq:impactParameter} b = \frac{L}{E}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-141: The radius of the shadow of the black hole, as seen by a distant observer, is obtained by evaluating this impact parameter at $r=r_\text{ps}$, i.e. \begin{equation}r_\text{shadow} = b(r_\text{ps}) = \frac{r_\text{ps} e^{\sigma(r_\text{ps})}}{\sqrt{N(r_\text{ps})}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-142: Figure \ref{fig:r_shadow shows the radius of the shadow for the SHBHs compared with the expected size for the shadow of a Schwarzschild black hole.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Notice that increasing the value of the parameter $\phi_0$ produces an increment in the radius of the shadow.

The dashed black line represents the radius of the shadow of a Schwarzschild black hole.} \label{fig:r_shadow}
elements.sent-2411-144: Distant observers usually measure the angular diameter of the shadow, defined by the relation \begin{equation}\Theta = \frac{2r_{\text{shadow}}}{D},\end{equation} where $D$ is the distance between the black hole and the observer.
elements.sent-2411-145: Giving $D$ in units of [Mpc] and introducing the mass of the black hole measured in solar masses, we obtain the angular diameter of the shadow in units of [$\mu$as] as \begin{equation}\frac{\Theta}{\text{ $\mu$as} }= \frac{6.191165 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi} \left(\frac{M}{M_\odot }\right) \left(\frac{D}{\text{Mpc}} \right)^{-1} \left(\frac{r_\text{shadow}}{M}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-146: Two interesting black holes may help to study the shadow size.
elements.sent-2411-147: The first one is Sgr A*, located at a distance of $D=8.127 \text{ kpc}$ and with a mass of $M = 4.14 \times 10^6 M_\odot$.
elements.sent-2411-148: The second one is M87*, located at a distance $D=16.8 \text{ Mpc}$ with a mass of $M = 6.2 \times 10^9 M_\odot$.
elements.sent-2411-149: The angular diameter of the shadows reported by the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) collaboration \cite{EHT1, EHT2 are $51.8 \pm 2.3 \text{$\mu$as}$ and $42 \pm 3 \text{$\mu$as}$ for Sgr A* and M87*, respectively.}

elements.subsubsection-2411-005: Energy Flux and the Image of the Accretion Disk
elements.sent-2411-205: Using the above results, we can obtain the image of a steady-state thin accretion disk on the equatorial plane of the SHBH.
elements.sent-2411-206: We will consider that the gas is in hydrodynamics equilibrium and described by the Page-Thorne model \cite{Page1974, Thorne1974 .}
elements.sent-2411-207: Hence, the energy flux emitted by the disk is given by \begin{equation}F_e = - \frac{\dot{M}}{4\pi \sqrt{-\gamma}} \frac{\frac{d\Omega}{dr}}{(E - \Omega L )^2} \int_{r_\text{ISCO}}^{r_s} (E-\Omega L) \frac{dL}{dr} dr,\end{equation} where $\dot{M}$ represents the accretion rate, $\gamma = - e^{2\sigma(r) r^4$ is the determinant of the induced metric in the equatorial plane, $L$, $E$ and $\Omega$ given by Eqs.~\eqref{eq:AngMomentumCircular , \eqref{eq:EnergyatCirc and \eqref{eq:OmegaatCirc , respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: This emitted energy flux will be affected by the redshift factor $g$ in Eq.~\eqref{eq:g_factor to give the observed energy flux, \begin{equation}F_o = \frac{F_e}{g^4}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-209: In Fig.~\ref{fig:accretionDiskFlux , we show the observed energy flux in the direct images of a thin accretion disk around SHBHs with different values of $\phi_1$ and $\phi_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-210: We consider the inclinations angles $\iota = 15^{\circ , 45^{\circ}$ and $80^{\circ}$.}
elements.sent-2411-211: The intensity of the energy flux is represented with a continuous color map, showing the existence of a brighter region on the left side of the disk caused by the Doppler effect produced by the gas rotation.
elements.sent-2411-212: The images also show that the intensity of the observed energy flux changes depending on the value of the parameters defining the black hole; i.e., $\phi_1$ and $\phi_0$.
elements.sent-2411-213: In this paper, we investigated the optical appearance of scalar hairy black holes with asymmetric potential, a numerical solution obtained in Ref.~\cite{Corichi:2005pa and discussed in Ref.~\cite{Chew:2022enh .}}
elements.sent-2411-214: To do so, we used the Hamiltonian formalism to obtain the equations of motion and define the potential for massive and massless particles (photons), from which we computed the photon sphere and the ISCO for different values of $\phi_1$ and $\phi_0$, parameters related to the asymmetric potential, see Eq.~\eqref{Sec_II_eq2 .}
elements.sent-2411-215: In the case of photons, our results show that the smaller the value of $\phi_0$, the closer to the Schwarzschild case.
elements.sent-2411-216: Moreover, the location of the photon sphere changes as $\phi_1$ and $\phi_0$ increase; the change is evident for values of $\phi_1>1.0$.
elements.sent-2411-217: In the case of $\phi_1=0.5$, the photon sphere maintains closer to that of Schwarzschild, i.e., $r_\text{ps /r_H\approx 1.5$.}
elements.sent-2411-218: On the other hand, for SHBH solutions with $\phi_1>1.0$, we found that the photon sphere decreases, reaches a minimum value, and then increases again as $\phi_0$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-219: When considering the ISCO, our results show a similar behavior: the smaller the values of $\phi_0$, the closer to the Schawarzchild black hole solution, being $\phi_0=0.5$ the closest, keeping the values of $r_\text{ISCO /r_H\approx 3.0$.}
elements.sent-2411-220: When $\phi_1$ and $\phi_0$ increase, our results show differences from Schwarzschild; however, we found that solutions with $\phi_1=2.0$ also keep the values of the ISCO close to that of Schwarzschild, see Fig.~\ref{fig:rISCO .}
elements.sent-2411-221: Similarly to the photon sphere, we also found that the ISCO reduced its value, reaching a minimum and increasing again as $\phi_0$ increases for values of $\phi_1>2.0$.
elements.sent-2411-222: In the case of the shadow, our analysis shows it increases as the value of $\phi_0$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-223: We found this behavior for all values of $\phi_1$.
elements.sent-2411-224: As a consequence, the angular diameter of the shadow increases for all values of the asymmetric potential parameters $\phi_1$ and $\phi_0$.
elements.sent-2411-225: In particular, the increment is prominent for SHBH solutions with $\phi_1=1.0$, see Fig.~\ref{fig:EHTshadows2 .}
elements.sent-2411-226: Using the values of the angular diameter as a function of $\phi_0$ for different values of $\phi_1$, we were able to constrain the asymmetric potential parameters.
elements.sent-2411-227: However, in the case of $\phi_1=0.5$, it was impossible to constrain the SHBH solutions since the numerical values did not reach the constraint set by the EHT observations for both M87* and SgrA*.
elements.sent-2411-228: Therefore, the case $\phi_1=0.5$ is excluded by EHT observations.
elements.sent-2411-229: In this work, we also investigated the profile of the specific intensity.
elements.sent-2411-230: In this case, we found an interesting behavior.
elements.sent-2411-231: Although the intensity $I_0$ has the usual profile (especially for small values of $\phi_0$), it becomes weaker when the value of $\phi_0$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-232: Moreover, our analysis shows that the photon sphere is sharper for small values of the $\phi_0$, while it blurs as $\phi_0$ increases and its size becomes large.
elements.sent-2411-233: In this sense, we can conclude that asymmetric parameter $\phi_0$ does affect the motion of photons, preventing them from circling the SHBH several times in contrast to the Schwarzschild case; this is shown clearly in Fig.~\ref{fig:shadows .}
elements.sent-2411-234: The iso-radial curves for the SHBH solutions have similar behavior as the Schwarzschild case; i.e., they all have the famous ``mushroom'' shape described by J.~P.~Luminet in Ref.~\cite{Luminet1979 .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Since the ISCO changes for different values of the asymmetric parameters $\phi_0$ and $\phi_1$, the size of the images is affected, growing for larger values of the ISCO.
elements.sent-2411-236: In the case of the primary image (continuous blue line in Fig.~\ref{fig:accretionDiskProfiles ), we see the usual behavior for different values of the inclination angle $\iota$.}
elements.sent-2411-237: Nevertheless, when analyzing the secondary image, we found differences.
elements.sent-2411-238: For example, for small values of $\phi_0$ and $\phi_1$, the secondary image (blue dashed line in Fig.~ref{fig:accretionDiskProfiles ) resembles that of Schwarzschild.}
elements.sent-2411-239: However, as the values of $\phi_0$ and $\phi_1$ increase, the upper part of the secondary image becomes flattened.
elements.sent-2411-240: This effect can be seen clearly in the last column, the fourth row of Fig.~ref{fig:accretionDiskProfiles ), where we show the SHBH solution with $\phi_0=1.0$ and $\phi_1=5.0$.}
elements.sent-2411-241: In the case of the redshift, similar to the Schwarzschild case, we see that the Doppler blueshift contribution due to the rotation of the disk in the left half-part of the observer's plate can exceed the overall gravitational redshift part due to the presence of the SHBH.
elements.sent-2411-242: Therefore, the corresponding photons have a resulting blueshift.
elements.sent-2411-243: In the right half-part, on the other hand, we see a resulting redshifting effect.
elements.sent-2411-244: The order of magnitude for the blueshift/redshift is the same for all the SHBH solutions.
elements.sent-2411-245: Hence, the asymmetric parameters, $\phi_0$ and $\phi_1$, do not affect the behavior of $z$.
elements.sent-2411-246: Using the values of $z$ obtained at each point in the observer's photographic plate, we computed the bolometric flux relative to the total factor $F_e$.
elements.sent-2411-247: Our analysis shows, as in the Schwarzchild case, that the flux is maximum in the regions where the spectral shift is a blueshift.
elements.sent-2411-248: On the other hand, we found the flux is affected by the parameters $\phi_0$ and $\phi_1$, and the inclination angle $\iota$.
elements.sent-2411-249: For example, when the inclination angle changes from $80^\circ$ to $15^\circ$, the flux decreases its value.
elements.sent-2411-250: We also found that the smaller values for the flux occur for the SHBH solution with $\phi_0=1.0$, $\phi_1=5.0$ and $\iota=15^\circ$, where the order of magnitude for the flux is $10^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-251: In contrast to the SHBH solution with $\phi_0=1.2$, $\phi_1=3.0$ and $\iota=80^\circ$, where the order of magnitude for the flux is $10^{-5}$.
elements.sent-2411-252: Finally, it is interesting to extend this work and consider axially symmetric black holes, which, from the astrophysical point of view, are more realistic and could help to test some black hole solutions in modified theories of gravity~\cite{Volkov:1998cc, Herdeiro:2014goa, Sullivan:2019vyi, Sullivan:2020zpf,Gao:2021ubl .}
elements.sent-2411-253: In particular, it would be interesting to consider the numerical solutions obtained by A.~Sullivan et al. in Refs.~\cite{Sullivan:2019vyi, Sullivan:2020zpf , where the authors developed a numerical code to obtain stationary and axisymmetric solutions that describe rotating black hole spacetimes in a wide class of modified theories of gravity, such as scalar–Gauss-Bonnet gravity with a linear (linear scalar–Gauss-Bonnet) and an exponential (Einstein-dilaton–Gauss-Bonnet) coupling.}
